volumes.name: SproutLab
volumes.description: Single-file PWA tracking feeding, sleep, growth, milestones, and medical data for Ziva. Wife Mode default. Hosted at rishabh1804.github.io/sproutlab/.
volumes.current_phase: Device Sync Reconcile — Session 1 spec R3-locked. Lyra to implement C1–C6 per spec §12. Empirical verification pending on FieldValue sentinel detection.

chapters.name: v1–v2 Iterative Builds
chapters.summary: Single-file HTML PWA built iteratively from v1.x through v2.38+. Core tracking: feeding, sleep, poop, growth, milestones, medical. Wife Mode, localStorage persistence, GitHub Pages hosting.
chapters.content:
## The Origin

SproutLab began in October 2025, a few weeks after Ziva was born on September 4th. The first version was a single HTML file — no framework, no build tools, no design system. Just a father’s instinct that if he didn’t start tracking now, the early months would blur into a fog of midnight feedings and half-remembered milestones.

The first feature was feeding tracking. Breast or bottle, which side, how long, what time. Simple forms, localStorage persistence, a table view. It worked. Within days, patterns emerged that weren’t visible to sleep-deprived intuition: Ziva fed more frequently in the evenings, her longest stretches between feeds were mid-morning, and the total daily intake was remarkably consistent even when individual feeds varied wildly.

## Growth Through Need

Each new feature was born from a real moment. Sleep tracking arrived when Rishabh couldn’t remember whether last night had two wakes or three — and the pediatrician asked. Poop logging started when the colors and consistencies began mattering diagnostically during a digestive concern. Growth measurements came before every doctor visit, because plotting weight and height over time told a story that a single measurement couldn’t.

By version 2.0, SproutLab tracked seven domains: feeding, sleep, poop, growth, milestones, activity, and medical. Wife Mode made it the daily driver for Ziva’s mother — a simplified interface optimized for the person who used the app forty times a day, not the person who built it.

## The Monolith Era

The architecture was simple by necessity. Everything in one HTML file meant no build step, no dependencies, no deployment complexity. Edit in any text editor, save, push to GitHub Pages. For a solo developer building between zinc plating shifts at the factory and midnight feedings at home, this simplicity was a feature, not a limitation.

But by early 2026, the monolith had grown past 55,000 lines. Adding a feature meant scrolling through thousands of lines of unrelated code. Debugging required holding the entire file’s structure in mental context. AI sessions could no longer fit the whole file — the context window wasn’t large enough. The architecture that enabled rapid early growth was now constraining it.

## What Survived

The core decisions of the v1–v2 era proved durable: localStorage over a backend (privacy, simplicity, offline-first), single-file PWA (zero deployment complexity), GitHub Pages hosting (free, reliable), and the principle that every feature must earn its place by solving a real problem that happened in the last 48 hours. These weren’t design principles yet — they were instincts. The design principles came later, when the instincts needed to be written down so they could survive beyond one person’s memory.

chapters.name: Architecture Split
chapters.summary: Split monolith into 9 modules (data, core, home, diet, medical, intelligence, start + styles.css, template.html). Concat build via build.sh. Phases 1–3 complete.
chapters.content:
## The Breaking Point

By April 2026, the SproutLab monolith had crossed 55,000 lines in a single HTML file. The breaking point wasn’t performance — the app ran fine. It wasn’t user experience — users never saw the code. The breaking point was developer experience, specifically the AI-assisted development workflow.

Claude’s context window couldn’t hold the entire file. Each session required uploading the file, but only a portion could be processed at a time. Making changes to the diet module required scrolling past 10,000 lines of unrelated sleep and medical code. A bug in the intelligence module meant searching through a haystack of 55,000 lines to find the relevant 2,000. The monolith that had enabled rapid early development was now the primary bottleneck to continued development.

## The Nine Modules

The split was surgical. The monolith was divided along domain boundaries into nine modules:

data.js: State management, localStorage persistence, data accessors. The foundation that every other module depends on.
core.js: Routing, rendering framework, navigation, header/tab bar. The application shell.
home.js: Dashboard, daily views, Today So Far. The landing experience.
diet.js: Food logging, Smart Quick Log, Food Favorites, nutrient intelligence. Everything about what Ziva eats.
medical.js: Vaccines, growth tracking, supplement adherence, Doctor Visit Prep. Everything about Ziva’s health.
intelligence.js: Cross-domain analysis, ISL, Smart Q&A, milestone tracking. The analytical brain.
start.js: Event delegation root, initialization sequence, gesture handlers. The boot code.
styles.css: All styling, design tokens, theme variables, component classes.
template.html: HTML shell, meta tags, manifest link.

Concatenation order matters — data before core before domain modules before start — because later modules reference functions and constants defined in earlier ones. build.sh enforces this order.

## The Build System

build.sh is a simple bash script that concatenates the nine modules in the correct order, wraps them in the HTML template with style and script tags, and outputs sproutlab.html. The workflow:

1. Edit module files in split/ directory
2. Run bash build.sh > sproutlab.html
3. Preview in browser
4. cp sproutlab.html index.html (GitHub Pages serves index.html)
5. git add, commit, push

A critical workflow rule, learned the hard way: always copy sproutlab.html to index.html before pushing. Forgetting this step means the live site stays on the old version while the source files update — a confusing state that has caused at least two debugging sessions.

## Chart.js Guards

The split introduced a subtle initialization issue: Chart.js was loaded once in template.html but referenced in multiple modules. If two modules both tried to initialize Chart.js on the same canvas, the library would throw. Guards were added to prevent double-initialization when modules load in sequence. The concat order is enforced by build.sh, not by convention, precisely because convention is fragile and the build system is not.

## What Changed, What Didn’t

The split changed the developer workflow fundamentally. Each session now uploads only the relevant module(s), not the entire codebase. Changes are scoped to the module that owns the feature. AI sessions can hold an entire module in context with room to spare.

What didn’t change: the user experience. The built output is still a single HTML file. The app still runs offline-first from localStorage. No dependencies were added, no framework introduced, no bundler required. The split was pure separation of concerns — the same code, organized for maintainability.

chapters.name: Diet Module
chapters.summary: Food logging, Smart Quick Log, Food Favorites, nutrient intelligence, ingredient picker.
chapters.content:
## From Breast to Bowl

Ziva was exclusively breastfed for her first six months. The diet module was built in February 2026, in anticipation of the transition to solids that began on March 6th with her first taste of khichdi. The module needed to solve a specific problem: tracking not just what Ziva ate, but how she responded to each new food — because food introduction in the first year is as much about monitoring reactions as it is about nutrition.

The food database started empty and grew with Ziva. Every new food was logged with its first introduction date, creating an adoption timeline. The system knew which foods were established (eaten multiple times with no reaction), which were in trial (introduced recently, still monitoring), and which were upcoming (recommended for her age but not yet tried).

## Smart Quick Log

The Smart Quick Log was the feature that made the diet module usable in practice. Before it, logging a meal required navigating through a form: select food, enter quantity, choose unit, set time, save. Seven taps minimum. For a parent who logs 6–8 meals a day while simultaneously feeding a squirming baby, seven taps per entry was a barrier to consistency.

Quick Log reduced it to three taps: food (from a ranked favorites list), quantity (pre-filled from history), done. The Food Favorites system learned from frequency — if Ziva had dal-rice at lunch every day, it appeared first in the list at lunchtime. Time was auto-filled to now. Quantity defaulted to the most common amount for that food. The result: under 5 seconds per log, which meant logs actually got made during the chaos of mealtime.

## Nutrient Intelligence

Beyond logging, the diet module built an intelligence layer. Nutrient heatmaps showed gaps across days — not just whether Ziva ate today, but whether her iron-rich food intake was consistent across the week. Combo frequency analysis identified which foods were always paired together (rice + dal, banana + yogurt) and which were never combined. Synergy detection correlated food combinations with outcomes in other domains — did high-protein dinners correlate with longer sleep stretches?

The ingredient picker made food entry precise without being tedious. A typeahead component indexed every food Ziva had ever eaten, ranked by frequency, with fuzzy matching. Type "kh" and khichdi appeared instantly. The picker powered both the logging form and search suggestions, making the entire diet interface feel responsive and personal.

## The Adoption Tracker

One of the quieter but most useful features: the food adoption tracker. It maintained a timeline of when each food was introduced, how many times it had been offered, and whether any reactions were noted. This answered the pediatrician’s most common question during the solid food phase: "What has she tried, and how did she do with each one?" Instead of guessing from memory, Rishabh could pull up a complete, timestamped history.

chapters.name: Medical Module
chapters.summary: Vaccination intelligence with reaction tracking and timeline visualization. Growth tracking, medical records.
chapters.content:
## Vaccination Intelligence

The medical module started as a simple vaccine log — date, vaccine name, done. But it evolved across Sessions A and B into a full intelligence system because vaccinations turned out to be one of the most anxiety-producing aspects of infant care, and data was the antidote to anxiety.

Reaction tracking captures post-vaccination symptoms with severity ratings (mild, moderate, concerning), duration in hours, and specific symptom types (fever, fussiness, injection site redness, sleep disruption). Over time, patterns emerged: Ziva consistently showed mild fussiness for 12–18 hours after combination vaccines but had no reaction to isolated ones. This data gave the parents confidence and gave the pediatrician actionable history.

The vaccination timeline visualization shows the complete history with upcoming doses highlighted based on the Indian immunization schedule. Each vaccine entry is color-coded by reaction severity. The timeline answers at a glance: what’s been given, what’s coming next, and what to expect based on past reactions.

## Growth Tracking

Growth measurements — weight and height — are plotted against time with growth velocity calculations. Velocity matters more than absolute values: a baby who’s consistently at the 25th percentile is healthy, but a baby who drops from the 50th to the 25th in two months needs attention. The velocity calculations detect acceleration and deceleration, flagging changes before they become clinically significant.

The module integrates with Doctor Visit Prep — a card that assembles relevant recent data into a summary ready for the pediatrician. Growth trends, vaccine reactions, active CareTickets, recent diet changes, and sleep pattern shifts, all in one view. No more scrambling to remember details in the doctor’s office.

## Supplement Adherence

Ziva takes Vitamin D3 supplements daily — standard for breastfed infants in India. The module tracks not just whether the dose was given (a binary that’s easy to forget by afternoon), but the exact administration time. Timing matters because D3 absorption is affected by proximity to feeding. The adherence view shows streaks and gaps, nudging consistency without guilt. A missed day is a data point, not a judgment.

## Why Medical Matters

The medical module represents something deeper than feature tracking. It’s the domain where data directly affects health decisions. Every other module — feeding, sleep, activity — is about optimization and awareness. Medical is about safety. The code reflects this: more validation, more null guards, more careful date handling than any other module. When the data is about a child’s health, there is no acceptable error rate.

chapters.name: Intelligence Module
chapters.summary: Cross-Domain Intelligence (6 cards), Smart Q&A Phase 2 (22 intents, 24 handlers).
chapters.content:
## The Analytical Brain

The intelligence module is where SproutLab stops being a logger and becomes an analyst. Individual domain modules — diet, sleep, medical — each track raw data in their own context. The intelligence module sits above all of them, finding patterns that no single domain could reveal.

The core insight driving the module’s design: baby care data is most valuable when domains talk to each other. A parent can see that Ziva slept badly last night. They can see that she ate less than usual yesterday. But without the intelligence module, they can’t easily see that she consistently sleeps worse on days when her protein intake is below a threshold — a correlation that only emerges when feeding and sleep data are analyzed together.

## Intelligence-Aware Scoring

Every day receives a composite score using an 80/20 base-modifier architecture. The 80% base comes from five domain scores: feeding consistency, sleep quality, digestion health, activity level, and medical status. Each domain score is normalized to 0–100 based on domain-specific criteria — feeding consistency rewards regular intervals and adequate volume, sleep quality measures total duration and wake frequency against age-appropriate baselines.

The 20% modifier adjusts for factors that span domains: trend direction (is this week better or worse than last week?), consistency (are the patterns stable or erratic?), and cross-domain correlations (does a change in one domain explain a change in another?). The modifier can boost a mediocre day that’s part of an improving trend, or flag a seemingly good day that breaks a concerning pattern.

A day where Ziva slept well, ate consistently, and had normal digestion scores higher than a day with one spectacular nap but erratic everything else. The system rewards stability over peaks because infant development thrives on predictable rhythms.

## The Intelligence Service Layer

The ISL is the computational engine behind the intelligence module. It provides temporal query parsing (understanding "last week" vs "the past 3 days"), domain data accessors (uniform interfaces to each module’s data), and day summary generation. The ISL powers both the intelligence cards on the home screen and the Smart Q&A system.

Key functions include generateRangeSummary() for multi-day analysis, getDomainScore() for individual domain evaluation, and detectCorrelations() for cross-domain pattern mining. The ISL was designed to be extended — adding a new analysis type means writing a new accessor function, not restructuring the pipeline.

## Evidence-Based Milestones

506 classification patterns across four developmental domains: motor (gross and fine), cognitive, language, and social-emotional. Each milestone has three states: upcoming (age-appropriate but not yet observed), emerging (partially demonstrated), and achieved (consistently demonstrated).

The auto-promotion engine watches activity logs and suggests milestone transitions. When Ziva started pulling to stand using furniture consistently, the motor milestone was promoted from emerging to achieved without manual intervention. The engine uses frequency thresholds — a behavior observed once might be a fluke, but observed five times across three days is a milestone.

The milestone system doesn’t diagnose or raise alarms. It tracks and visualizes. The interpretation belongs to parents and pediatricians. SproutLab provides the data; humans provide the judgment.

chapters.name: Smart Q&A
chapters.summary: Phase 2: 22 intents, 24 dedicated handlers, natural language query processing for baby data.
chapters.content:
## Natural Language for Baby Data

Smart Q&A exists because data is only useful if you can ask it questions. The intelligence module generates insights proactively, but parents also need to ask specific questions: "When was her last feed?" or "How has her sleep been this week?" or "What foods correlate with good naps?"

Phase 1 introduced basic query parsing. Phase 2, built across Sessions A and B, expanded to 22 intents with 24 dedicated handlers, transforming Smart Q&A from a search feature into a conversational data interface.

## The 22 Intents

Queries are classified into four categories. Lookback intents retrieve historical data: "How did Ziva sleep last week?" triggers a range summary with duration averages, wake counts, and trend direction. Pattern intents find recurring behaviors: "What’s her feeding pattern on weekdays?" analyzes interval regularity and volume consistency across weekday data. Planning intents project forward: "When should her next vaccine be?" checks the immunization schedule against completed doses. Comparison intents evaluate change: "Is she eating more than last month?" computes period-over-period deltas with statistical context.

Each intent maps to a dedicated handler. The handler doesn’t just return data — it adds context. Ask about sleep duration and you get the raw hours, but also how it compares to her 7-day average, whether the trend is improving or declining, and what the age-appropriate benchmark is. Ask about feeding and you see today’s total alongside the weekly pattern, with any significant deviations highlighted.

## Query Parsing

The temporal query parser understands relative time references: "yesterday," "last week," "the past 3 days," "since Monday," "this month." It also handles domain-specific vocabulary: "feeds" maps to the diet module, "naps" maps to daytime sleep entries, "shots" maps to vaccinations. Compound queries like "how many times did she wake last night?" are decomposed into domain (sleep), metric (wake count), and timeframe (last night = previous sleep session).

The parser uses a keyword-matching approach rather than NLP — no model, no API calls, everything runs locally in the browser. This keeps it fast and offline-capable. The tradeoff is that creative phrasings sometimes miss, but the system handles the 90% case well and degrades gracefully on the rest.

## Graceful Degradation

When a query doesn’t match any intent, the system says so honestly rather than guessing. The response includes a suggestion of similar queries that would work, helping the user learn the system’s vocabulary organically. Partial matches get clarification prompts: "Did you mean sleep duration or sleep quality?" This approach builds trust — parents learn to rely on the answers because the system never fabricates one.

chapters.name: Search Bar Upgrade
chapters.summary: ISL generateRangeSummary(), lookback/pattern/planning query modes, nutrient intelligence cards, ingredient picker.
chapters.content:
## From Filter to Intelligence

The Search Bar Upgrade, built across Sessions A and B, transformed search from a simple text-matching filter into an intelligent query routing system. Before the upgrade, search was a text input that filtered visible list items. After, it became the primary entry point for the Intelligence Service Layer.

## Three Query Modes

The upgraded search bar routes queries into three modes based on intent analysis. Lookback mode handles historical queries: "How did Ziva sleep last week?" triggers ISL’s generateRangeSummary() to produce a structured analysis of the requested time range. Pattern mode handles recurring behavior queries: "What’s her usual morning routine?" aggregates data across multiple days to find regularities. Planning mode handles forward-looking queries: "When is the next vaccine due?" checks schedules and projections.

Each mode produces a different response format. Lookback returns a summary card with metrics and trend arrows. Pattern returns a rhythm visualization showing typical daily sequences. Planning returns a timeline with upcoming events and recommended actions.

## Nutrient Intelligence Cards

When search queries touch diet data, the results include nutrient intelligence cards. Ask about iron intake and you see a heatmap of iron-rich food frequency over the past two weeks, not just a list of matching log entries. Ask about "new foods" and you get the adoption tracker view showing recently introduced foods with their reaction status. The search bar becomes a lens into whatever domain the query touches.

## The Ingredient Picker

The ingredient picker is a typeahead component that powers both the diet logging form and search suggestions. It knows every food Ziva has ever eaten, ranked by frequency and recency. The ranking is contextual — at 8 AM, breakfast foods rank higher; at noon, lunch foods rise. The picker indexes on first load and filters entirely in memory, so there is no perceived delay even on slower devices.

Fuzzy matching handles misspellings and abbreviations. Type "daal" or "dal" or "dhal" and the same item appears. The picker also understands food categories: searching "fruit" surfaces all logged fruits even if the exact term "fruit" was never used as a food name.

## ISL Integration

The search bar is the user-facing surface of the Intelligence Service Layer. Behind the scenes, ISL’s temporal query parser decomposes queries into timeframes, domains, and metrics. The domain data accessors pull data from the appropriate module. The day summary generator compiles cross-domain overviews. The search bar simply presents the results — the intelligence runs deeper.

chapters.name: Today So Far
chapters.summary: Smart card with vertical chronological list, warmth gradient bar, pattern-based nudges, 12 functions with _tsf* prefix.
chapters.content:
## The Question Every Parent Asks

Today So Far exists to answer the question every parent asks a dozen times a day: "How’s the day going?" Not how did the last feed go. Not how long was the nap. The holistic question: taking everything together — feeding, sleep, activity, digestion, mood — is today a good day or a hard day?

The smart card renders a real-time summary of everything that has happened since midnight, designed to be understood in a single scroll on a phone screen.

## The Vertical Timeline

The card’s core is a chronological list of today’s events: feeds, sleeps, diaper changes, activities, medications, milestone observations. Each entry shows the time, a domain-colored icon, and a brief description. The list reads like a diary of the day, written in data rather than words.

A warmth gradient bar at the top gives an at-a-glance quality score. The gradient shifts from cool (blue-gray) through warm (amber) to vibrant (gold) based on the composite day score from the intelligence module. Parents learn to read the bar intuitively: a quick glance at the color tells them whether the day is tracking well before they read any details.

## The Horizontal Timeline Rejection

The original design proposed a horizontal scrolling timeline with events plotted along a time axis. This was rejected decisively (documented as rej-0001) because a 375px phone screen made horizontal scroll unusable for dense data. The timeline would show 3–4 hours at a time, requiring constant scrolling to see the full day. The vertical chronological list won on readability, scannability, and the simple fact that vertical scrolling is the natural gesture on a phone.

## Pattern-Based Nudges

The card doesn’t just report — it nudges. If Ziva hasn’t eaten in 3 hours and her usual interval is 2.5, a gentle prompt appears: "It’s been a while since the last feed." If her nap is running 30 minutes longer than her average, the card notes it neutrally: "Nap running long today." If the daily feeding volume is tracking below her weekly average, a soft flag appears.

These nudges are pattern-based, not rule-based. They learn from Ziva’s actual rhythms rather than imposing textbook schedules. A baby who naturally feeds every 3.5 hours doesn’t get nudged at 2.5 hours just because a chart says so. The system adapts to the child, not the other way around.

## The Spec Journey

The Today So Far spec went through 4 review rounds and reached 533 lines before it was declared build-ready. 12 functions carry the _tsf prefix. The card rebuilds on every data change via the onChange listener. It was one of the most heavily specified features in SproutLab history, because getting a daily summary wrong means parents distrust the whole app. If the card says "good day" when the day feels hard, or flags a concern when everything is fine, it loses credibility permanently.

chapters.name: CareTickets
chapters.summary: 21-field data model, 7 templates, 6-transition state machine, main-thread notifications, illness-aware metric pausing, TSF integration, share summary. Spec v5 (~1,089 lines). 15 QA rounds, 310 scenarios.
chapters.content:
## The Biggest Build

CareTickets was SproutLab’s most ambitious feature. Four build phases (A through D), a 1,089-line spec refined through five versions, 15 QA rounds covering 310 test scenarios, and an 8-pass SPEC_ITERATION_PROCESS that caught 8 critical bugs before a single line of code was written. It exists because baby care concerns don’t fit neatly into feeding logs or sleep charts.

When Ziva runs a fever, the question isn’t "how much did she eat today?" — it’s "how is the fever progressing, what other symptoms are appearing, when should we call the doctor, and what happened last time she was sick?" CareTickets tracks the concern itself: its lifecycle, its symptoms, its trajectory, its resolution.

## The Data Model

21 fields per ticket capture the full anatomy of a concern. The core fields: type (from 7 templates: fever, rash, teething, digestive, respiratory, injury, general), severity (mild/moderate/concerning/urgent), onset time, primary symptoms, secondary symptoms, associated domains (which feeds or sleeps were affected), temperature readings (for fever tickets), medication administered, notes, and resolution details.

The 6-transition state machine governs the ticket lifecycle: Draft (capturing initial observations) → Active (concern confirmed, monitoring initiated) → Monitoring (stable but watching) → Resolved (concern passed). Two branches: Paused (temporarily set aside, e.g., during travel) and Escalated (severity increased, doctor contacted). Each transition is timestamped, creating a complete audit trail.

## The Spec Process

The CareTickets spec went through the full 8-pass SPEC_ITERATION_PROCESS. Pass 1 (Concept) defined the problem space. Pass 2 (Data Flow) mapped every data path. Pass 3 (Integration) identified touchpoints with other modules. Pass 4 (Bugs Today) enumerated every edge case — what happens when two tickets are active simultaneously? What if a ticket spans midnight? What if the user creates a ticket, closes the app, and returns three hours later?

Pass 5 (Drift Bugs) caught the subtle ones: what if a future module change breaks a CareTickets assumption? Pass 6 (Builder Questions) listed every decision the implementation would need to make. Pass 7 (Consistency) verified the spec aligned with the design principles. Pass 8 (Completion) confirmed the spec was build-ready — meaning a builder could implement it without making a single undocumented decision.

8 critical bugs were caught in the spec process before coding began. This validated the entire SPEC_ITERATION_PROCESS methodology.

## Notification Architecture

Phase D introduced notification-driven follow-ups. When a fever ticket is active, the system nudges every 4 hours to log a temperature update. When a rash ticket is monitoring, it prompts daily for a progress photo comparison.

The notification architecture decision was significant enough to become canon-0045. Service Worker push notifications were rejected (documented as rej-0002) because they require a backend server, VAPID keys, are unreliable on mobile browsers due to battery optimization, and fail silently. The chosen approach: main-thread notification scheduling via setTimeout. Simple, predictable, works as long as the PWA tab is open — which it always is, since SproutLab runs as an installed PWA.

## Illness-Aware Metrics

When a CareTicket is active, the entire intelligence system adjusts its expectations. A sick day’s sleep score isn’t compared against healthy baselines — it’s compared against illness baselines. Feeding drops during illness don’t trigger deficit alerts. Activity scoring suspends. The Today So Far card shows active tickets prominently at the top, reframing the entire day around the active concern.

Share Summary generates a WhatsApp-ready text for the pediatrician: ticket type, duration, symptom timeline, temperature readings, medications given, and current status. One tap to compose, one tap to send. Because when your child is sick, the last thing you want is to type a medical history into a chat window.

## The Personal Dimension

CareTickets was built during the week Ziva had her first real illness — a combination of teething pain and a mild respiratory infection that disrupted sleep, reduced appetite, and produced a low-grade fever. The spec was written from lived experience. Every field exists because a real conversation with a pediatrician needed that data point. The state machine mirrors how a parent’s concern actually evolves: initial worry, active monitoring, gradual improvement, cautious resolution.

It’s the feature where SproutLab stopped being a tracker and became a care tool.

chapters.name: Bug Capture System
chapters.summary: Three capture paths, WhatsApp/clipboard delivery.
chapters.content:
## The Feedback Loop Problem

SproutLab’s primary daily user is Rishabh’s wife. She uses the app dozens of times a day — logging feeds during nursing, checking sleep duration during naps, updating the diet log at mealtimes. When something breaks, the feedback loop needs to be instant and frictionless. A formal bug tracking system (Jira, GitHub Issues, even a simple form that posts to a server) would never be used. The cognitive cost of switching contexts, describing the bug in a structured format, and submitting it through a formal channel is too high when you’re holding a baby in one arm.

The Bug Capture System was built to match how bug reports actually happen in a family: a quick message on WhatsApp.

## Three Capture Paths

Path 1 — Deliberate Report: A button in Settings opens a bug report form. Title, description, steps to reproduce (optional), current view, device info. This is for bugs noticed during calm moments — "that chart looked wrong earlier" or "the date picker shows the wrong month."

Path 2 — Quick Capture: A long-press on the FAB (Floating Action Button) triggers an immediate capture. The form pre-fills with the current view, timestamp, and device info. The user just needs to type what went wrong. This is for bugs caught in the moment — "I just tapped this button and nothing happened."

Path 3 — Automatic Capture: window.onerror catches JavaScript errors and presents a pre-filled bug report with the error message, stack trace, and current state. The user can add context ("I was trying to log a feed when this happened") and submit. This catches the bugs the user didn’t even notice — silent failures, uncaught exceptions, rendering errors.

## Delivery Channels

Bug reports are formatted for two delivery channels: WhatsApp and clipboard. The WhatsApp format generates a pre-composed message with the bug details, ready to send to the developer. The clipboard format copies a structured text that can be pasted anywhere — a message, a note, an email.

No server is involved. No database stores the bugs. No ticket system tracks their lifecycle. The entire bug pipeline is: capture on device → format as text → deliver to WhatsApp. The developer (Rishabh) receives the bug report in the same conversation where he already talks to his wife about Ziva. The bug becomes part of the family’s daily communication, not a separate workflow.

## Why This Matters

The Bug Capture System embodies a design philosophy that runs through all of SproutLab: tools should match the user’s actual behavior, not impose an idealized workflow. Bug tracking systems are designed for engineering teams. SproutLab’s bug reporter is designed for a mother who noticed something odd while nursing at 2 AM. The right tool for that context is a WhatsApp message, and the system respects that reality.

chapters.name: Cross-Domain Intelligence
chapters.summary: 6 intelligence cards spanning feeding, sleep, growth, and medical domains.
chapters.content:
## The Connective Tissue

Cross-Domain Intelligence represents a fundamental insight about baby care data: the most valuable patterns live between domains, not within them. A parent can look at the feeding log and see what Ziva ate. They can look at the sleep log and see how she slept. But the question they actually want answered — "did what she ate affect how she slept?" — requires connecting the two.

The six cross-domain intelligence cards were designed to surface these connections automatically, presenting correlations that parents sense intuitively but cannot prove without systematic data.

## The Six Cards

Sleep-Feeding Correlation tracks the relationship between feeding timing and sleep quality. Does a late evening feed predict night wakes? Does feed volume correlate with sleep duration? The card computes lagged correlations between feeding events and subsequent sleep sessions, highlighting statistically meaningful patterns.

Diet-Digestion Patterns connects food intake to digestive outcomes. Which foods correlate with changes in poop consistency or frequency? This is particularly important during the solid food introduction phase, when each new food is a potential digestive experiment. The card maintains a food-reaction matrix that updates with every new data point.

Growth-Feeding Alignment compares caloric intake trends against growth velocity. Is Ziva eating enough to sustain her current growth rate? Is there a gap forming between intake and growth that might indicate a feeding issue or a growth spurt that needs more calories? The card shows both curves on a shared timeline.

Activity-Sleep Impact examines whether high-activity days produce different sleep patterns. Do days with more physical activity (tummy time, crawling practice, standing attempts) correlate with longer total sleep, more consolidated sleep, or more restless sleep? The answer varies by age and developmental stage.

Vaccination Recovery tracking shows how post-vaccine days differ from baseline across all domains. Feeding often dips, sleep may be disrupted, fussiness increases. The card quantifies the recovery curve: how many days until all domains return to baseline after each vaccine type?

Daily Rhythm Analysis presents a holistic view of the day’s pattern — when does Ziva’s energy peak, when does she need rest, when are the optimal windows for solid food introduction? The rhythm evolves as she ages, and the card tracks this evolution.

## Design Philosophy

Every card follows the same design contract: a headline insight in plain language, a supporting data visualization (chart, heatmap, or trend line), and a one-sentence interpretation. No card requires scrolling to understand its main point. No card uses statistical jargon. The audience is a tired parent at 2 AM who needs signal, not noise. The cards are glanceable because glanceability is a form of respect for the user’s time and cognitive load.

chapters.name: Migration M1–M3
chapters.summary: M3: emoji→SVG, inline style extraction, onclick→delegation. Full codebase modernization.
chapters.content:
## The Debt Collector

Migrations M1 through M3 were the unglamorous but essential work of paying off technical debt. The SproutLab codebase had grown organically through v1 and v2, with conventions evolving over time. Code written in October 2025 followed different patterns than code written in March 2026. The design principles existed in v3, but the code predated them. Migrations enforced the Hard Rules retroactively on every line.

## M1: The Emoji Purge

Every emoji in the codebase — hundreds of them scattered across buttons, headers, status indicators, and labels — was replaced with a zi() SVG icon call. This was not a cosmetic preference. Emojis render differently across Android versions, iOS versions, and browsers. A baby bottle emoji on a Samsung Galaxy looks nothing like the same emoji on an iPhone, and both look different in Chrome versus Firefox. zi() icons render identically everywhere because they are inline SVGs defined in the codebase itself.

The purge required creating new zi() icons for every emoji use case, mapping each emoji to its semantic equivalent, and verifying that no emoji survived in any string literal, comment, or data constant. The result: a visually consistent application regardless of device.

## M2: Inline Style Extraction

Hundreds of inline style attributes — the accumulated shortcuts of a fast-moving solo developer — were extracted into CSS classes using the design token system. Every `style="padding: 8px"` became `class="sp-8"`. Every `style="font-size: 14px"` became a reference to `--fs-sm`. Every `style="border-radius: 12px"` became `class="r-lg"`.

The extraction was mechanical but required judgment. Some inline styles were one-offs that legitimately didn’t warrant a class. These were evaluated case by case: does this style represent a pattern? If yes, extract. If it’s truly unique, document why it remains inline. In the end, fewer than ten inline styles survived, each with a comment explaining its exception.

The payoff was immediate: changing `--sp-8` in the :root now updated every 8px gap in the application. Theme switching became trivial. Text size scaling worked uniformly. The codebase was no longer a collection of independent styling decisions but a system.

## M3: Event Delegation

Every onclick attribute in the HTML — every `onclick="handleFeedLog()"` and `onclick="toggleMenu()"` — was replaced with data-action delegation. A single event listener on the document body catches all clicks, reads the `data-action` attribute, and routes to the appropriate handler. This pattern, borrowed from modern frameworks but implemented in vanilla JS, gave three benefits.

First, greppability: searching for an action name in the codebase finds every place it’s used and the single place it’s handled. Second, no memory leaks: dynamically created elements don’t need individually attached listeners. Third, consistency: every interactive element follows the same pattern, making the codebase predictable.

## What Changed

The three migrations took multiple sessions of careful find-and-replace with manual verification. No feature was added. No UI changed. No user noticed. But the codebase was transformed from code that happened to work into code that was designed to be maintained. The migrations proved the principle that later became canon-0025: split architecture from day one. SproutLab’s monolith migration was painful enough that every subsequent project (SEP Dashboard, SEP Invoicing, Codex) started with split modules and build.sh from the first commit.

chapters.name: Design Principles v3
chapters.summary: 2,011 lines locked. Overlay/Chip/Card/Answer Card/Form systems plus Animation Tokens, Touch Targets, data-action naming.
chapters.content:
## The Constitution

At 2,011 lines, Design Principles v3 is not a style guide — it is a constitution. It codifies every visual pattern, interaction paradigm, component specification, and naming convention that governs SproutLab’s codebase. It was written not as an aspirational document but as a retroactive lockdown: after the codebase reached sufficient maturity, the principles were extracted from what worked and declared law.

The document covers overlay mechanics (open/close animations, backdrop behavior, escape key handling, scroll lock), chip component specs (height, padding, border radius, active states, domain color integration), card layouts (header/body/footer structure, meta-line patterns, expandable sections), answer card templates (for intelligence module results), form field specifications (label positioning, validation states, error messages), animation tokens, touch targets (minimum 44px), and the data-action delegation naming convention.

## The Twelve Hard Rules

HR-1 through HR-12 are the non-negotiable rules that every line of SproutLab code must satisfy:

HR-1: No emojis. All iconography uses the zi() SVG icon system. Emojis render inconsistently across devices and cannot be themed.
HR-2: No inline styles. All styling through CSS classes and custom properties. This ensures consistency and theme-ability.
HR-3: No inline event handlers. All interactivity via data-action delegation. A single delegation root makes behavior predictable and debuggable.
HR-4: escHtml() at all render boundaries. Every user-supplied string is sanitized before innerHTML insertion. Non-negotiable security practice.
HR-5: All spacing, font sizes, and radii via CSS tokens. No magic numbers. --sp-*, --fs-*, --r-* tokens exclusively.
HR-6: data-action naming follows verb-noun or domain-verb-noun convention. Every action name is greppable.
HR-7: zi() returns SVG HTML strings, set via innerHTML. Never textContent (which would render SVG as text).
HR-8: Stub features show "Coming soon" via showQLToast(). No dead buttons, no silent no-ops.
HR-9: Post-build multi-round QA audit. Continue until only cosmetic bugs remain.
HR-10: No text-overflow ellipsis. Truncation hides meaning. Always find a layout solution.
HR-11: Math.floor for all currency values. Never round up on money.
HR-12: Timezone-safe dates via localDateStr(). Never raw ISO date slicing.

## Why Constitutions Matter

The Hard Rules are not bureaucracy. They are the reason a solo developer can maintain a 55,000-line codebase built across hundreds of AI sessions without it collapsing into inconsistency. Every new session starts by reading the design principles. Every build ends by auditing against them. Every QA round checks HR compliance.

The rules also serve as institutional memory. When Aurelius starts a new session with incomplete context, the design principles document tells it exactly how SproutLab code should look, behave, and be structured. Without this document, each session would reinvent conventions, introduce subtle inconsistencies, and slowly erode the codebase’s coherence.

## The Domain Color System

SproutLab uses a six-color domain system: rose (feeding), indigo (sleep), peach (poop/digestion), amber (activity/milestones), sage (medical/growth), lavender (intelligence). Each domain color has light and dark mode variants, is used in chips, status indicators, chart series, and section headers. The domain colors are the visual language that lets a parent glance at a screen and immediately know which aspect of Ziva’s care they’re looking at.

chapters.name: Device Sync Reconcile
chapters.summary: R3-locked spec for Session 1 of device sync: reconcile greenfield + four-bug remediation. Three architectural decisions (setDocMerge wrapper, event-loop-is-the-lock, ingress discrimination) reduce the surface from the handoff's estimated +900–1100 LOC to ~211 LOC net. HR-14 candidate Hard Rule governs reconcile's sync invariant. S7 concurrent-write race accepted as known limitation.
chapters.content:
## The Phase 2 Gap

SproutLab's sync engine shipped in April 2026 as Phases 1 and 2 of the original DEVICE_SYNC_SPEC. Phase 1 delivered auth and household management. Phase 2 delivered the write-and-listen loop — syncWrite routing user changes to Firestore, onSnapshot listeners mirroring remote changes back into localStorage. The spec's §7.5 described a third piece, the Reconcile Path, for resolving offline-made local changes against remote state after listeners re-settle. That piece was never built.

Phase 2 left `_syncIsReconciling` declared at sync.js line 20. No code path ever set it to true. The flag survived as a dead hook — its only consumers used it to suppress toast notifications during a reconcile that never fired. A fossil of intent.

For several weeks the gap was invisible. In normal use — single device, always-online, small household — the listener loop alone was sufficient. The bug class that reconcile exists to prevent only surfaces when two devices diverge offline and then reconnect. The first device-sync user was the project's creator, working solo.

## The Four Bugs

Field use and spec review surfaced four issues that needed addressing before reconcile could be built on top of a load-bearing foundation. Three of them pointed at undefined-field semantics in different boundary positions:

**P1R2-2.** When SYNC_KEYS values contained undefined fields — milestones entries with `manualAt: undefined` was the canonical case — the JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(val)) pattern in the sync path produced inconsistent results. Firestore rejected some payloads outright and silently omitted others, creating false-positive change detection and latent shape drift. The write path needed a normalization boundary.

**P3R4-4.** Reconcile, when built, would push payloads to Firestore through a direct setDoc call — bypassing any normalization applied at save(). The P1R2-2 class would reappear at reconcile time unless normalization was structural rather than distributed.

**P4R5-7.** The ingress mirror of the same problem. The single-doc listener handler at sync.js:730 treated remote-null as "nothing to write, skip." For keys where null carried semantic weight — `vaccBooked = null` means a parent cancelled the vaccination booking — peer cancellations were invisible to the receiving device. Parent B could drive to an appointment Parent A had already cancelled.

**P3R4-2.** A different shape of bug entirely. If reconcile called save() inside its merge loop, triggerAutosave would fire mid-merge, and a later restoreAutosave would observe a snapshot that was half-pre-merge and half-post-merge. A user-facing integrity bug not just a correctness one.

## The R3 Reframe

The handoff letter that opened Session 1 described these as four bugs to patch. Round 3 of the iteration revealed the framing was wrong on two counts.

First, only two of the four targeted code that existed. P1R2-2 and P4R5-7 had real patch sites in core.js and sync.js. P3R4-2 and P3R4-4 targeted the reconcile function — which hadn't been written yet. They were not patches. They were design constraints on greenfield code.

Second, the three undefined-semantics findings were not one root cause. Egress — sending local values to Firestore — needed to destroy the undefined-vs-null distinction by normalizing undefined to missing-field. Ingress — receiving Firestore values into local — needed to preserve the absent-vs-present-null distinction so peer cancellations could propagate. A single normalizer could not do both jobs; they were asymmetric. One architectural primitive on the egress side, one discrimination helper on the ingress side.

These reframings collapsed the scope. The handoff's estimated +900–1100 LOC diff became ~211 LOC net. The Edict III open question — whether reconcile's push should route through save() — dissolved once _syncSetDocMerge became the architectural normalization boundary. The correctness concern was handled by the wrapper; what remained was a latency question about debounce, answered immediate.

## Event-Loop-Is-The-Lock

The deepest design move in Session 1 concerned reconcile's isolation from the listener loop. Seven scenarios were traced — the intersection of listener-echo and reconcile under various orderings. Two surfaced the same class of bug: a listener firing during reconcile's merge could write to local state that reconcile was about to read or overwrite. Specifically, a peer's vaccBooked cancellation could arrive via listener during A's reconcile, get written to local, and then be clobbered by reconcile's subsequent push of the pre-cancellation value. P4R5-7's fix — make the cancellation visible — would be defeated by the race.

The initial proposal (Option A) was an explicit listener queue: while `_syncReconcileWriting` was true, listener callbacks would enqueue their snapshots rather than execute; reconcile's finally block would drain the queue on exit. This worked but added complexity: queue data structure, drain logic, bounded-size considerations, crash-recovery semantics.

Option B was more elegant. JavaScript's single-threaded execution model already provides the isolation. A listener's snapshot callback cannot fire while synchronous code is on the stack. If reconcile's read-compute-write-kickoff sequence is fully synchronous — no await, no Promise chains, no setTimeout, no yielded callbacks — then no listener can interpose. The event loop is the lock. No queue is needed because listener callbacks queue naturally at the task-queue layer and fire when reconcile's synchronous region completes.

The constraint this imposed — HR-14, the Reconcile Sync Invariant — is more demanding than it first appears. Any future work that wants to await a fetch, chain a then, or schedule a timer inside the locked region breaks the invariant and silently reintroduces the race. The spec marks HR-14 as a Hard Rule candidate, to be promoted to committed status by Lyra after Session 1 lands.

## The Normalization Wrapper

`_syncSetDocMerge` became the architectural centerpiece. Three lines:

```
function _syncSetDocMerge(ref, payload) {
  return ref.set(_syncNormalizeOut(payload), { merge: true });
}
```

Every payload-bearing remote write in sync.js routes through it. An audit surfaced thirteen Firestore write sites across sync.js; five carry user payloads and route through the wrapper, one uses inline normalization for its update-with-sentinels shape, and seven are household metadata or pure deletions that don't need normalization at all. The audit classified each, producing a migration map that made the rewire a mechanical exercise rather than a judgment call per site.

`_syncNormalizeOut` itself handles the FieldValue sentinels that Firestore's compat SDK uses for server timestamps and field deletions. These are object-shaped but carry internal markers (`_methodName` on the compat SDK) that the walker must detect and pass through untouched. The detection is one of two items in the spec that require empirical verification at implementation time — the compat SDK's internals are not formally documented, and a major SDK upgrade could change the marker.

## The Residual

One scenario (S7) was traced and accepted rather than solved. When two devices write to the same key concurrently — Parent A reconciles with a pending vaccBooked, Parent B cancels vaccBooked at the same moment — Firestore's last-write-wins at the server decides the outcome. If A's push lands later, B's cancellation is lost. This is inherent to Firestore's optimistic-write model and solving it requires versioned writes or CRDT data structures, both of which are out of Session 1's scope.

The residual was documented, a user-facing mitigation note was drafted for future SproutLab user docs ("if you cancel a booking while another family member is offline, confirm with them after they reconnect"), and the spec moved on.

## What Session 1 Leaves

The spec locks at R3 with ~211 LOC net surface: six small primitives, one architectural wrapper, five rewired call sites, one ingress patch, and a ~170-LOC reconcile module built to HR-14's invariant. The commit sequence is laid out in §12 of the spec as six discrete commits, ordered so Maren's review — scoped to payload-normalization and null-semantics correctness — can examine C3 and C4 in isolation.

The handoff letter that kicked off Session 1 needs a §4/§5 rewrite before the Builder opens it; its four-bug framing no longer matches what Session 1 actually builds. The spec's §15 captures the delta. Whether the handoff is rewritten, or the spec's §15 serves as the bridging document, is a choice for the Architect.

What Session 1 leaves unfinished is deliberate: month partitioning, per-entry meaningful-null, versioned writes, observability metrics, and per-date bucketing for activityLog merges. Each is a well-shaped future spec; none is Session 1 surface.

The thread beneath all of it is that Ziva's care record is capital, and sync is the plumbing that keeps it coherent across the two people who read it. The technical framing — event loops, FieldValue sentinels, union merges — is all in service of one property: when a parent cancels an appointment, the other parent sees the cancellation, reliably, in every scenario the architecture can protect against. The ones it can't — S7's narrow race — are named and documented so the user can compensate.

chapters.name: v1–v2 Builds
chapters.summary: Iterative build from v1.x through v2.0. Attendance, production logging, stock tracking, payroll with wage calculation engine.
chapters.content:
## The Origin

SEP Dashboard started in early 2024 as a pragmatic response to a daily problem: tracking attendance, production, and costs for a zinc electroplating plant with 21 workers across permanent and contract categories. The first version was a single HTML file — a digital logbook replacing the paper registers that had been the operational backbone of Soma Electro Products for decades.

The initial build tracked attendance marking (present, absent, half-day, leave), daily production in pieces processed, and a basic cost summary. All data lived in localStorage, keyed by date. The interface was functional but rough — inline styles, emoji icons, terse function names like gk() and sS() and qM(). It worked on the factory floor, which was all that mattered.

## v1 Through v9: Feature Accumulation

Over 2024, the app grew version by version. Stock tracking arrived — chemical inventory (zinc anodes, passivation solutions, cleaning agents) with consumption logging. Payroll followed, with a wage calculation engine that handled the complexity of Indian labour law: permanent staff with basic/HRA/DA splits versus contract workers paid by attendance. OT calculations, advance management, pay period selectors.

By v9, the codebase had accumulated significant technical debt. 645 inline styles, 162 onclick handlers, 64 emojis used as icons, and terse variable names that made the code unreadable to anyone — including its author — after a few weeks away. The app was powerful but brittle.

## The v1.4 Rebuild

In early 2026, informed by the hard lessons of SproutLab's architecture migration (which had cost three painful sessions to eliminate similar debt), the decision was made: rebuild from clean foundations rather than patch the accumulated mess.

v1.4 was not a feature addition — it was a constitutional rewrite. A 7-principle design system was established, adapted from SproutLab's mature design principles. A 9-domain color system gave each functional area its own semantic palette: attendance green, cost amber, production blue, danger red, warning yellow, neutral brown, contract worker green, permanent worker blue. All coded as CSS custom properties, dark-mode ready.

Hard Rules HR-1 through HR-8 were codified — no emojis (zi() icon system instead), no inline styles (CSS tokens only), no inline handlers (data-action delegation), escHtml() at all render boundaries. Every principle that SproutLab had learned the hard way was applied proactively.

The rebuild preserved all functionality while establishing the architectural foundations for future growth. At 3,190 lines, v1.4 was cleaner, more maintainable, and extensible. The feature gap list from v9 (calendar grids, pay week selector, data migration, OT editing, advance management) became the Phase 2.0 roadmap — to be ported feature by feature, each time cleaning the debt in the area it touched.

## Design Philosophy

The design principle that defines SEP Dashboard: it is used on a phone in a zinc electroplating plant. Bright sunlight, greasy fingers, at a glance. Every decision — touch target sizes, contrast ratios, information density — serves that context. The app is not trying to be beautiful; it is trying to be readable at arm's length by a supervisor checking today's attendance count between plating bath inspections.

chapters.name: SEP Restructuring
chapters.summary: Effective 1 Apr 2026. DIGITAL-001 v5, KB-006 AppSheet spec, KPI-001–007 binders done. Salary confirmations complete. Data reconciliation in progress.
chapters.content:
## The Constitutional Moment

On 1 April 2026, Soma Electro Products underwent its first formal operational restructuring. What had been an informally managed zinc electroplating job-work operation — running on verbal agreements, handwritten registers, and inherited practices — was given a written constitution.

The restructuring was not triggered by a crisis. It was triggered by clarity: after building digital tools (the Dashboard, the Invoicing PWA) that made operational data visible for the first time, it became obvious that the underlying policies, roles, and financial frameworks had never been documented. You cannot digitize what you have not defined.

## The Document Estate

34 documents were produced across 9 policy families: POL (6 operational policies), HR (8 human resource documents), FIN (5 financial frameworks), SAFE (4 safety and compliance documents), IT (1 information technology policy), DIGITAL (1 digital platform specification), ORG (1 organisational structure), CON (2 contracts), and KB (5 knowledge base guides). Every document was designation-based and tool-agnostic — policies define what must be done and why; they survive platform changes.

The 8-pass SPEC_ITERATION_PROCESS, originally developed for SproutLab feature specs, was adapted for policy writing. Each document went through concept, integration, consistency, and completion passes. The result: zero ambiguity on roles, responsibilities, and procedures.

## Key Decisions

Three salary negotiations were completed and confirmed: Production Supervisor at Rs 17,280, Pickling Section at Rs 13,200, and VAT Area A1 at Rs 15,000. The combined permanent staff budget came to Rs 2,87,649 per month against a target of Rs 2,60,000 — a conscious decision to invest in retention over strict budget compliance.

The salary component structure was locked at 50/20/20/10: Basic, Fixed Benefit Allowance, Performance, and Attendance incentive. This structure ties 30% of compensation to measurable outcomes — a deliberate shift from the previous flat-wage model.

## Digital Infrastructure

DIGITAL-001 v5 specified the digital operations platform: a hybrid of AppSheet (17-table schema for daily operations), Google Sheets (15 interconnected sheets for data aggregation), and the existing PWA Dashboard for real-time visibility. The platform was designed to replace paper registers entirely within a 14-day deployment window after KB-006 approval.

KB-006, the AppSheet specification document, defined every table, field, validation rule, and user permission. It was the bridge between policy (what should happen) and technology (how it happens digitally).

## Financial Analysis

The restructuring surfaced a critical finding: SSS Mehta, accounting for roughly 40% of revenue, was loss-making at Rs 5.40/kg against a true cost of Rs 5.46/kg. Rate negotiation analysis showed Rs 5.70-5.80/kg as the optimal anchor. Chemicals consumed approximately 30% of revenue, staff approximately 31%, leaving an operating margin of roughly 31%.

These numbers had always existed in the business — but until the restructuring forced them into documented frameworks, they had never been visible in one place.

## What Remains

The restructuring is largely complete but not finished. Data reconciliation between the old paper systems and the new digital infrastructure is ongoing. The Shyam operations and culture discussion guide — a follow-up meeting to align the senior-most operational staff with the new frameworks — remains a pending TODO. Five of eight salary conversations have been concluded; the remaining three await scheduling.

Time-bound: 30 days from Republic v1.0 ratification (2026-04-19), i.e., by 2026-05-19. Import Republic document by reference; document Dashboard palette (nine-domain grid, palette-collision resolution via §10.2 three-tier tree), icon catalog (data-domain glyphs per §6 data-domain requirement), entity types (plant lines, bath chemistry, temperature zones, throughput metrics), HR-11 currency surface for rate calculations.
